figures.caption: Figure 1: Qualitative results generated with FLUX.1-dev [3] ((a) NFE=5, (b) NFE=9).
Figure 2: Qualitative results generated with SANA-0.6B [4] (NFE=5).

Figure 3: 3D ODE analysis of the span limitation. (A) We use the Halvorsen system as a controlled nonlinear ODE and study a one-step update from ti = 1.8 to ti+1 = 2.0. (B) With K = 2, the buffered velocities {ui, ui−1} induce the local span Vi. The scalar-coefficient updates in (a) Adams– Bashforth [37] and (b) DyWeight [19] remain inside Vi, whereas (c) SpanLift can move outside the span. (C) The squared update mismatch decomposes as in (7). Compared with (a) and (b), (c) moves closer to the best in-span projection ΠVi∆ ⋆ i , reducing the in-span mismatch. For a fixed state and velocity buffer, the out-of-span residual ∥r⊥i ∥2 is a lower bound on the local error of any scalar-coefficient update. By moving beyond Vi, SpanLift can reduce this residual along directions unavailable to any scalar combination.

Table 2: Image generation with pixel space diffusion model. FID↓ [40] results across four datasets, including (a) CIFAR-10 [41], (b) ImageNet [42], (c) FFHQ [43], and (d) AFHQv2 [44]. We compare SpanLift with handcrafted multi-step solvers and learnable solvers.

Figure 5: Comparison on step-wise x̂1 estimation. Starting from the same seed, DyWeight misses the key object specified in the prompt, whereas SpanLift corrects the later trajectory and produces a clear clock face. Prompt: “A large clock on the side of a building above cars on the street.”
